title: Chrysanthemum morifolium (Ju Hua)
medicine: Ju hua (Chrysanthemum morifolium) has many health benefits, such as reducing fever, calming the liver, alleviating anxiety and depression, improving digestion, and promoting weight loss. It is commonly used to treat various conditions, including colds, flu, headaches, and dizziness.

The medicinal constituents of Ju hua include flavonoids, terpenoids, and triterpenoids. Flavonoids are antioxidants that help protect cells from damage by free radicals. Terpenoids are anti-inflammatory compounds that can reduce pain and swelling. Triterpenoids have anti-bacterial properties that can help fight off infections.

The most commonly used parts of Ju hua include the flowers, leaves, and stems. Flowers are often used to make teas, while leaves and stems can be used to make decoctions or extracts. In traditional Chinese medicine, Ju hua is often combined with other herbs to enhance its medicinal properties.

While Ju hua is generally considered safe, it may cause some side effects in rare cases. These include nausea, vomiting, dizziness, and headaches. In addition, people with allergies to chrysanthemums should avoid using Ju hua supplements or teas.

To use Ju hua safely, it is important to follow the recommended dosage and consult a healthcare professional before taking any supplements. Pregnant women and children should not take Ju hua without first speaking to their doctor. Additionally, people with liver disease should exercise caution when using Ju hua, as it can interact with certain medications and cause liver damage in high doses.
horticulture: Chrysanthemums (Ju Hua) require full sun to partial shade for optimal growth. They prefer well-drained soil with a pH range of 6.0 to 7.5. Ju Hua plants grow best when their roots are kept moist throughout the summer, but not waterlogged. The plants should be pruned regularly to promote bushy growth and remove dead or damaged foliage.

Ju Hua should be planted in early spring after the last frost date. Choose a location with good drainage and full sun exposure. Space the plants about 30 cm apart to allow for proper air circulation and sunlight penetration. Water the plants thoroughly after planting, but avoid overwatering.

Ju Hua requires regular watering throughout the growing season, especially during dry spells. The soil should be kept evenly moist, but not waterlogged. Prune the plants regularly to promote bushy growth and remove dead or damaged foliage. Fertilize the plants with a balanced fertilizer every 4-6 weeks during the growing season.

Ju Hua blooms in late summer and early fall, typically from August to October. Cut off the flower heads as soon as they open to encourage more flowers to develop. The plants can be harvested up until the first frost date. Dry the flower petals by tying them in small bundles and hanging them upside down in a well-ventilated area for several days.

Ju Hua is susceptible to several pests, including aphids, spider mites, and whiteflies. These can be controlled with insecticidal soap or neem oil. The plants may also develop fungal diseases such as powdery mildew or leaf spot if the soil is not well-drained or if the foliage is kept wet for too long. To prevent these diseases, ensure good air circulation and proper drainage around the plants, and prune away any infected foliage.
botany: Ju Hua, with botanical name Chrysanthemum morifolium, belongs to the domain Eukarya, kingdom Plantae, phylum Tracheophyta, class Magnoliopsida, order Asterales, family Asteraceae, genus Chrysanthemum, and species morifolium. It is commonly known as "Chinese Aster," "Japanese Flower," "Flower of the Four Seasons," "Sweet William," "White Flower," "Four-Season Flower," "Eternal Flower," and "Golden Flower."

Ju Hua is a perennial herb that grows up to 1.5 meters tall with a rosette of compound leaves and a stem covered in small, sharp hairs. The flowers are large and showy, measuring up to 8 centimeters in diameter. They come in various colors such as white, pink, red, purple, and blue, and have a daisy-like shape with a flat, discoid central disk surrounded by ray florets. The plant produces both male and female flowers on the same stem, and the fruit is an achenium that turns red or orange when ripe.

There are several variants of Ju Hua, including the single-flowered type, the double-flowered type, the rosebud type, the button type, and the miniature type. The single-flowered type has one flower per stem, while the double-flowered type has two flowers per stem. The rosebud type is characterized by its buds that resemble rosebuds, and the button type has small, compact flowers. The miniature type is a dwarf variety that grows up to 30 centimeters tall and produces tiny flowers.

Ju Hua is native to East Asia and is widely cultivated in Japan, China, Korea, Taiwan, and Vietnam. It grows well in temperate and subtropical regions with full sun exposure and well-drained soil. The plant can also be found in natural habitats such as forests, grasslands, and riversides, where it grows as a weed or an invasive species.

Ju Hua has a life cycle that spans over three years. In the first year, the plant produces rosettes of leaves from its seed and develops a stem. In the second year, it produces flower buds and flowers on the stem. In the third year, the plant produces fruit and seeds that are dispersed by animals and wind. The seeds germinate in the following spring, and the life cycle repeats itself.
